label: Design
tagline: Blueprint the learning experience before building a single asset.

whatItIs.beginner: Design is where you plan your training before you build anything. You take what you learned in Analysis and turn it into a blueprint — deciding what learners will know and do by the end, how you'll teach it, how you'll test it, and what the course will look like. Think of it like an architect drawing plans before construction starts.
whatItIs.advanced: The Design phase operationalises your Analysis findings into a formal instructional strategy. You write measurable performance objectives using a framework like ABCD (Audience, Behavior, Condition, Degree), sequence content using task analysis and prerequisite mapping, select instructional strategies aligned to Bloom's taxonomy levels, and define your assessment strategy (criterion-referenced tests, performance tasks, etc.). You also produce the Design Document or Blueprint — the contract between you and your stakeholders before development begins.

whyItMatters.beginner: Designing before building saves enormous time and rework. If you start developing in PowerPoint or Articulate without a plan, you'll likely rebuild slides when stakeholders change direction. A design document gives everyone a chance to agree on direction before you invest hours of development work.
whyItMatters.advanced: The Design phase creates alignment between learning objectives, instructional strategies, and assessment — the 'golden thread' of instructional design. Without it, you risk developing content that doesn't map to objectives, assessments that don't measure the right behaviors, and a sequence that doesn't account for prerequisite knowledge. The Design Document also functions as a scope management tool, making it clear what is and isn't in the course.

keyQuestions: What will learners be able to do differently after this training? (Terminal objectives)
What sub-skills do learners need to achieve the terminal objectives? (Enabling objectives)
How will we assess whether learners have met the objectives?
What instructional strategies are best suited to these objectives and this audience?
What is the optimal sequence of content?
What media and format will we use and why?
What level of Bloom's taxonomy are these objectives targeting?
outputs: Learning Objectives (terminal + enabling) in ABCD format
SME review and sign-off on objectives

commonMistakes.fix: Use observable, measurable verbs. If you can't see or hear the learner doing it, it's not a good objective. Use Bloom's verb lists.
Every assessment item should map to a specific objective. If you can't trace it back, cut it.

commonMistakes.mistake: Designing for the wrong Bloom's level
commonMistakes.fix: Check the actual job task. Most workplace training needs Apply or Analyze level thinking — not just Remember.

commonMistakes.mistake: Skipping the design document and going straight to development
commonMistakes.fix: A one-page design document forces alignment before expensive development work begins. Even a brief outline beats no plan.

commonMistakes.mistake: Letting SMEs dictate content quantity instead of objectives
commonMistakes.fix: Anchor every content decision to an objective. If content doesn't support an objective, cut it.

advancedInsights.heading: When a full design document is overkill
advancedInsights.body: For small, urgent one-off requests, replace the full design document with a one-page 'design decision memo': objectives, assessment approach, delivery format, and estimated scope — bullet format, reviewed in a 15-minute call. This gets stakeholder alignment without the overhead of a formal blueprint. Reserve full design documents for programs over 30 minutes of content or any project with multiple stakeholders who need traceability.

advancedInsights.heading: Sequencing in Articulate Rise vs. Storyline
advancedInsights.body: In Articulate Rise, your content structure must be fully planned before you open the tool — Rise's linear section structure is difficult to restructure mid-build. Map your sequence in Airtable, Notion, or even a Word outline before authoring. In Storyline, slide order is more flexible, but scene organization done upfront prevents variable and trigger naming conflicts that compound across large projects.

advancedInsights.heading: Regulatory training: the traceability matrix
advancedInsights.body: For regulatory or compliance training (financial services, healthcare, food safety), your design document may need a traceability matrix — a table mapping each policy citation or regulatory requirement to the objective that covers it, the content that teaches it, and the assessment item that tests it. This gives auditors proof that every requirement is addressed, and gives SMEs a structured way to verify coverage during review.

advancedInsights.heading: See also: Dick & Carey Systems Approach
advancedInsights.body: Dick & Carey's model prescribes more rigorous steps for objective writing and task analysis than standard ADDIE Design — particularly around subordinate skill identification and developing assessments before instructional materials. For high-stakes certification programs or when you need to prove instructional rigor to an accrediting body, consider adopting their approach for this phase.

caseStudy.situation: With Analysis complete, the Meridian team had a clear performance gap but needed to translate it into an instructional architecture before anyone opened an authoring tool.
caseStudy.approach: The terminal objective was written as: "Given a customer inquiry, sales reps will select the correct product configuration and explain its three key differentiators with 90% accuracy on a written scenario assessment." Targeting Bloom's Apply and Analyze levels — not just Remember — ruled out a simple quiz-based course. Content was sequenced from product families → individual specifications → competitive comparisons → customer application scenarios. The assessment strategy paired scenario-based multiple-choice questions with a manager observation checklist for the optional VILT component. The design document also included a traceability matrix mapping each objective to its content, assessment item, and evaluation measure.
caseStudy.outcome: The design document surfaced a scope conflict: the sponsoring stakeholder originally wanted all 14 product families covered. The traceability matrix made it clear that only 4 families mapped directly to the terminal objective and the lost-deal data from Analysis. The remaining 10 were documented as deferred scope for a Phase 2 project — a decision that saved an estimated 6 weeks of development time and prevented the team from building content no one needed for the identified problem.
